judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 is stronger overall: it matches Report 1 on root cause, commit attribution, and primary remediation, but additionally adds the application-level tenant-filter defense-in-depth layer (expectation d) that Report 1 explicitly argues against; Report 1's boot-time check correctly targets ownership (matching expectation e), while Report 2's check (rolsuper/rolbypassrls) would not actually have caught this specific ownership-based bypass.

report_1.evidence: Postgres row-level security has a documented exception: RLS policies are never applied to the table owner, no matter how the policy is written, unless the table is additionally altered with FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY... juniper_owner, and juniper_owner is the same role that created the appointments table

report_2.evidence: In Postgres, ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY does not apply RLS to the table's owner (or to superusers) — only to other roles — unless FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is also set. Since juniper_owner is the role that created appointments... any connection authenticated as juniper_owner is exempt from the tenant_isolation policy entirely

report_1.evidence: The vulnerability was introduced in commit b2a23d2 ("ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot", 2026-06-17), which changed all three DATABASE_URL config sources from the low-privilege runtime role to the owner role... in .env.example, docker-compose.yml, and infra/main.tf

report_2.evidence: It became exploitable at commit b2a23d2 — "ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot"... The diff changed all three deployment surfaces in one commit: --- a/.env.example ... --- a/docker-compose.yml ... --- a/infra/main.tf

report_1.evidence: Stop the runtime from authenticating as the owner. Revert DATABASE_URL in juniper/docker-compose.yml:5, juniper/infra/main.tf:2, and juniper/.env.example:1 back to the juniper_app role... Make RLS mandatory regardless of role... add ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

report_2.evidence: Revert DATABASE_URL in juniper/.env.example:1, juniper/docker-compose.yml:5, and juniper/infra/main.tf:2 back to juniper_app... Add ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; immediately after the ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY line.

Per project documentation's guidance, also add an application-level tenant filter (WHERE tenant_id = :tid) to the query in juniper/backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19 as a second, independent layer, so isolation doesn't depend solely on the DB policy firing correctly.

report_1.evidence: a startup assertion in db.py/deps.py that queries current_user/SELECT rolname FROM pg_roles WHERE oid = pg_table_owner(...) and refuses to boot if the runtime role equals the table owner.

report_2.evidence: Add a boot-time assertion in the backend (e.g., in db.py or an app startup hook) that queries rolsuper/rolbypassrls for current_user and refuses to start (or logs a stop-the-line alert) if the connected role can bypass RLS.
